ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for cheese-topped flatbread or ekmek; closest parent 'bread food product' (FOODON_00002683) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E 'Bread and similar products' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for cheese ekmek exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 'Bread (food)' is the closest parent; no specific concept for cheese-topped ekmek exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of cheese ekmek is approximately 120 g, representing one piece of cheese-topped bread as commonly served at a Middle Eastern restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese bread (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — composite estimate from white bread and mozzarella cheese)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry for cheese ekmek exists. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for white bread (FDC ID 325871, approximately 75% by weight) and part-skim mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 170845, approximately 25% by weight), reflecting a homemade bread topped with shredded mozzarella. Energy, macros, and micronutrients are weighted composites rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese ekmek. GI estimated at 70 based on white bread (GI=70, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. The mozzarella cheese topping contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially lower the GI of the bread base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 46.2) / 100 ≈ 32.4, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 38.5 × 1.2 = 46.2 g.

hypertension.guidance: Cheese ekmek contains approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (576 mg per 120 g serving), which represents roughly 25% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 70 and a glycemic load of approximately 32 per standard serving, cheese ekmek can cause a rapid postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size, pair with low-GI foods or protein, and monitor blood glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 5.2 g saturated fat and 576 mg sodium; while moderate consumption is unlikely to be harmful in the context of an otherwise heart-healthy diet, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of cumulative saturated fat and sodium intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Ekmek, also known as Peynirli Ekmek in Turkish, is a Middle Eastern-style cheese bread consisting of a soft, leavened wheat bread base topped generously with shredded mozzarella cheese and baked until the cheese is melted and lightly golden. The word 'ekmek' means 'bread' in Turkish, and this preparation is a popular street food and restaurant item across Turkey and the broader Middle Eastern region. In Singapore, it is served at Middle Eastern restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant at Boat Quay. The bread base is soft and slightly chewy with a mild yeast flavour, while the mozzarella topping adds a creamy, milky, and mildly salty character with a stretchy texture when warm. Nutritionally, cheese ekmek is a moderate-to-high calorie food providing approximately 271 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 11.2 g per 100 g from both the bread and the cheese. It is a notable source of calcium (approximately 195 mg per 100 g) and provides moderate amounts of B vitamins from the enriched wheat flour. The sodium content is relatively high at approximately 480 mg per 100 g, and the glycemic index is high (approximately 70), making it a food that individuals with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally considered halal. It is not suitable for those with gluten or dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese ekmek?
Is cheese bread good for diabetics?
What is the GI of cheese bread?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for cheese ekmek as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for white bread and part-skim mozzarella cheese in approximate proportions consistent with the dish description. GI is estimated from the white bread analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for white bread (FDC ID 325871) and part-skim mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 170845) in a 75:25 weight ratio, reflecting a bread-dominant preparation with a cheese topping. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for both white bread and mozzarella cheese — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from the USDA SR Legacy composite and are not -1 sentinel values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record. GI estimated at 70 from white bread analogue (University of Sydney GI Database). The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Grains and staples' as the dominant component is bread (wheat flour), with cheese as a topping. The input cuisine tags (Shawarma, Kebab) are not directly relevant to this dish and have been noted but not carried forward as primary classification. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy the Singapore restaurant context in which this dish is served; Turkish romanized form 'Peynirli Ekmek' is included as the dish's origin-language name.
